documents.title: Ohio Notary Course Packet
documents.description: Primary packet and source-of-truth study guide for the Ohio notary course.
documents.recommendedUse: Use this first every day. Track your resume page here and treat it as the main source document.
documents.previewText:
Notary Public
Guidebook
and
Study Guide
www.BecomeAnOhioNotary.com
info@BecomeAnOhioNotary.com
330.436.0118
©2025 - Ohio Notary Services, LLC
Introduction
This guidebook is intended to provide you with a
general overview of how to become and serve as a
notary public. A notary public is a public officer
appointed by the Ohio Secretary of State to serve as
an impartial witness to a transaction.
As a notary you will be able to administer oaths, take
and certify acknowledgments (e.g., deeds, mortgages,
liens, powers of attorney and other instruments of
writing), and depositions.

documents.title: Notary Course Notes
documents.description: Condensed notes from the course with high-frequency exam facts and reminders.
documents.recommendedUse: Use after the main packet when you want a faster review pass.
documents.previewText:
1
Notary Course Notes Notary Public 101 • A notary: A Notary Public in Ohio is a public officer commissioned by the Ohio Secretary of State to act as an impartial witness to the signing of legal documents, administer oaths, and certify affidavits. They are authorized to prevent fraud by verifying the identity of signers and serve a five-year term (except for attorneys). • Impartiality: means that notaries have “no interest” in the document content, other than ensuring it is complete. Scan do not read the document to ensure it is complete. If there are any blank spaces, the signer must either complete the areas/line through them. Ohio law 1. OH Constitution 2. OH Revised Code 3. OH Administrative code The OH Secretary of State • Processing of notary public • Issue all notary commissions • Maintains an online database of notaries public • Authorizes educational providers • Investigates possible violations & take disciplinary action Duplicate Commission • Upon receipt of a fee of $2 and submission of the electronic duplicate request form, a duplicate request form as notary public shall be issued by the secretary of state • Upon receipt of a fee of $2 + submission of the electronic amendment form of a name change under division (B) of section 147.05 of ORC, the secretary of state shall issue a duplicate commission as a notary public • Exception One: an attorney admitted to the practice of law in OH by the supreme court of OH, and the person’s principal place of business/has the persons primary practice in OH they meet the qualification of residency • Exception Two: a person that’s an attorney admitted to the practice of law in OH/peace officer is not required to submit a criminal redcords check when applying to be appointed a notary public • Attorney Qualifications: o An attorney admitted to the practice of law in OH by the supreme court of OH
2
o A principal place of business/the persons primary practice in OH o Complete an education program Public Record? • A criminal records check is not a public record • The following can access the report: o Person who’s the subject of the criminal records check/the persons representative o The OH secretary of state and their staff; o And a court, hearing officer, or other necessary individual involve in a case dealing with within a case denial resulting with the criminal background check • Peace officers BCI: peace officers are required to upload their OPOTA Certificate to the OH secretary of state o ORC – Section 2935.01: defines a “peace officer.” - Term of Notary Commission • Each notary public shall hold office for term of 5 years unless commission is revoked • Exception (for attorneys): admitted to the practice of law in OH by the supreme court of court holds office as notary public as long as: o The person’s principal place of business/the person’s primary practice in ohio o Attorney’s in good standing beforeth Amendment of Notary Public Info • Change of name/address/notice of resignation/or conviction of disqualifying offenses Notaries public database Located on the OH Secretary of State Website Database provides the following information on notaries:
3
Record- Keeping • Journal – keep journal in case of subpoenas in court; occasionally during court proceedings, notarized documents are challenged. o Notaries public may receive a subpoena & testify in court. While notaries aren’t required to keep a journal, it’s a best practice • Example of Notary Journal Entry:
Notary Public Fees • Notarial Act fee: Up to $5 for any notarial act that is not online notarization. o Notary public fees are not cumulative; fee cannot be calculated on a per signature basis. • Online notarization fee – up to $30 • Prohibited from charging both fees for the same act & prohibited from charging/receiving excessive fee. • Additionally, notaries may charge an reasonable travel fee, agreed on by the notary and the signer prior to notarial act. • Commission can be permanently revoked if notary public overcharges. • Tech fees =up to $10 Notarial Acts • Means act which laws & regulations of this state authorize notaries public of this state to perform.
4
• Notarial Acts – ORC 147.51: o The administration of oaths & affirmations. o Take and certify acknowledgments and deeds, mortgages, liens, powers of attorney, & other instruments of writing. o Taking depositions • Notarial Acts/Authorized Person – ORC 147.52: o When proof of authorized person’s authority is needed o Foreign proof o Clerk of Court of Record o Title & Signature • Notarial Steps 1. Verify the principal’s identity 2. Create a journal entry 3. Administer the notarial acts 4. Complete the notarial certificate 5. Collect fee • When notarizing a document o Excluding online notarization, does the principal have to personally appear before a notary public in order to have the document notarized? § Yes! No matter what the circumstance the principal is required to personally appear before the notary public in order to have document notarized. Also, the principal is required to provide proper identification. o Verifying Identity: § Identification must contain a persons photo/signature § Must be current or less than 3 years expired § Satisfactory to the notary public o Failure to verify identification may result in fraud, liability issues, and/or monetary damages • Designated alternative signer: an individual whose physical characteristics limit the individuals ability to sign a document presented for notarization may direct a designated alternative signer to sign on the individuals behalf • Signature by mark: Physical disabilities/other characteristics can render a signer unable to sign except a simple “X” o NOTE: § Signer must be able to make the mark on their own § Signer must make the mark with the “intent to sign” Notarial Certificate • Means the part of, or attachment to, a document that’s completed by the notary public and upon which the notary public places the notary public’s signature and seal

documents.title: Ohio Notary Exam Study Guide v2
documents.description: Cleaned exam-ready study guide built from transcript content and organized for recall.
documents.recommendedUse: Use for fast review, memory aids, and weak-topic repair before quizzes.
documents.previewText:
Ohio Notary Exam Study Notes and Study Guide
Cleaned and organized from the uploaded course transcripts, with official Ohio Secretary of State verification where useful.
Prepared from
Uploaded transcript files (filtered to substantive notary content only)
Scope
Detailed notes, exam outline, procedures, memory aids, and practice questions for Ohio
Verified current through
April 7, 2026
Use this as a study guide, not as legal advice. The guide tracks the course transcript, but any live work as a notary should be checked against the Ohio Revised Code and Ohio Secretary of State resources.
1. How this guide was built
This document consolidates the substantive Ohio notary information discussed in the uploaded course transcripts into a cleaner, exam-ready format. Non-substantive interruptions, profanity, and unrelated speaker dialogue were intentionally excluded. Where transcript material touched on rules that are sensitive to legislative change, the guide was cross-checked against current Ohio Secretary of State pages so your notes are usable for present-day studying.
2. Fast exam facts
The transcript states that the required exam is 30 multiple-choice questions.
The transcript states that a score of at least 80% is required to pass.
The required subject areas include: the terms of commission, renewal, revocation, legal notarial acts, depositions, acknowledgments, and jurats.
Exam domain
What to know cold
Commission basics

documents.previewText:
Audio file
RPReplay_Final1775579559.m4a
Transcript
Speaker 1
list customarily used as a source of such information. If the notarial act is performed by a person other than one described in divisions A and B of this section, there is sufficient proof of the authority of that person to act if the clerk of a court of record in the place in which the notarial act is performed certifies to the official character of that person and to that person's authority to perform the notarial act. The signature and title of the person performing the act are prima facie evidence that the person is a person with the designated title and that the signature is... You may be wondering what the notarial steps are. Here they are. One, verify the principal's identity. Two, create a journal entry. Three, administer the notarial acts. Four, complete the notarial certificate. Five, collect a fee. When notarizing a document, excluding online notarizations, does the principal have to personally appear before a notary public in order to have a document notarized? The answer to this question is yes. Excluding remote online notarizations, the principal is required to personally appear before the notary public in order to have the document notarized. Also, they must provide proper identification. When notarizing a document, failure to verify identification may result in fraud, liability issues, and/or monetary damages. Identification must contain a person's photo or signature. It also must be current or expired less than three years. The answer to this question is yes. Excluding remote online notarizations, the principal is required to personally appear before the notary public in order to have the document notarized. Also, they must provide proper identification. When notarizing a document, failure to verify identification may result in fraud, liability issues, and/or monetary damages. Identification must contain a person's photo or signature. It also must be current or expired less than three years. And it has to be satisfactory to the notary public. A notary public has personal knowledge of the identity of the person appearing before the notary public if the person is personally known to the notary public through dealing sufficient to provide reasonable certainty that the person has the identity claimed. either of the following document notarized? The answer to this question is yes. Excluding remote online notarizations, the principal is required to personally appear before the notary public in order to have the document notarized. Also, they must provide proper identification. When notarizing a document, failure to verify of the individual, which is current or expired not more than three years before performance of the notarial act, and is satisfactory to the notary public. by verification on oath or affirmation of a credible witness personally appearing before the notary public and personally known to the notary public, or whom the notary public can identify on the basis of a passport, driver's license, or other government-issued non-driver identification card, which is current or expired not more than three years before performance of the notarial act. A witness is not credible if the witness has a conflict of interest regarding the transaction. A notary public may require a person to provide additional information or identification credentials necessary to assure the notary public of the identity of the person. As used in this section, conflict of interest means either of the following: person has a direct financial or other interest in the transaction in question. The person is named individually or as a grantor, grantee, mortgager, mortgagee, trustor, trustee, beneficiary, vendor, lessor, or less or as a party in some other capacity to the transaction. An individual whose physical characteristics limit the individual's ability to sign a document presented for notarization may direct a designated alternative signer to sign on the individual's behalf. if all of the following are met. The individual clearly indicates through oral, verbal, physical, electronic, or mechanical means to the notary public the individual's intent for the designated alternative signer to sign on the individual's name on the notarial document. Both the individual and the designated alternative signer provide satisfactory identification to the notary public. The designated alternative signer signs the document in the presence of the notary public. The designated alternative signer is not named in the document. The notarial certificate provided to the individual gives the name of the designated alternative signer and states that the document was signed under this section at the direction of the individual. An individual may use a designated alternative signer to perform an online notarial act if all of the requirements of this section are met. Another method of a signature is a signature by MARC. Physical disabilities or other characteristics can render a signer unable to sign except with a simple X or other identifying mark. In these cases, this does not mean that the person does not have sufficient capacity to execute a document. The Ohio Revised Code does not require additional witnesses or signature by mark. Please note, neither the notary nor a third party may physically hold or guide the signer's hand to help them make the mark. Just to refresh your memory, a notarial certificate means the part of or attachment to a document that is completed by the notary public and upon which the notary public places the notary public's signature and seal. A notarial certificate may be provided through any of the following means: reprinting on a notarial document, ink stamp, handwritten note, a separate attached document. A notarial certificate shall show all of the following information: The state and county venue where the notarization is being performed. The wording of the acknowledgement or jurat in question. The date on which the notarial act was performed. The signature of the notary exactly as shown on the notary's commission. The notary's printed name displayed below the notary's signature or inked stamp. and the notary's notarial seal and commission expiration date. If an electronic document was signed in the physical presence of a notary and notarized pursuant to section 147.591 of the revised code, or if an online notarization was performed pursuant to sections 147.60 to 147.66 of the Revised Code. The certificate shall include a statement to that effect. Please note, the notarial certificate determines the type, either an acknowledgement or jurat, of notarial act. Later in the course, we will teach you how to easily identify the type of the wording of the notarial certificate. A notary public shall provide a completed notarial certificate for every notarial act the notary public performs. If a notarial certificate incorrectly indicates the type of notarization performed, the notary public shall provide a correct certificate at no charge to the person signing in question. A notary public shall not use an acknowledgement certificate with regard to a notarial act in which an oath or affirmation has been administered. A notary public shall not use a jurat certificate with regard to a notarial act in which an oath or affirmation has not been administered. A jurat certificate shall state that an oath or affirmation was administered to the signer with regard to the notarial act. A notary public may explain to a signer the difference between an acknowledgement and a jurat, but shall not, unless that notary is an attorney, advise the person on the type of notarial act that best suits a situation. Next, I want to show you two examples of wording of notarial certificate. The key words are acknowledge and act and deed. These notarial act examples allow us to explore acknowledgments in jurats. Let us start with acknowledgments. The form of a certificate of acknowledgment used by a person shall be accepted in Ohio if the certificate is in a form prescribed by the laws or regulations of the state, The certificate is in a form prescribed by the laws or regulations applicable in the place in which the acknowledgement is taken. Or the certificate contains the words acknowledged before me or their substantial equivalent. Here is an example of a jurat. The keyword on this example is duly sworn. Other keywords for jurats include are subscribed and sworn. sworn to, being dual sworn, jurat, or affirmed. The next example is of an acknowledgment. An acknowledgment means a declaration by an individual before a notary public that the individual has signed a record for the purpose stated in the record. And if the record is signed in a representative capacity, that the individual signed the record with proper authority and signed it as the act of the individual or entity identified in the record. A person taking an acknowledgment shall certify that the person acknowledging appeared before the notary public and acknowledged that they executed the instrument.
Speaker 1
And the person acknowledging was known to the person taking the acknowledgment, or that the person taking the acknowledgment had satisfactory evidence that the person acknowledging was the person described in, and who... Can we let her out there?
Speaker 1
Notaries public will ask if the acknowledgment is the principal's free act and deed.
Speaker 1
The notary public shall provide a completed notarial certificate.
Speaker 1
Every notarial act the notary public performs.
Speaker 1
If the notarial certificate incorrectly indicates the type of notarization performed The notary public shall provide a correct certificate at no charge to the person signing in question.
Speaker 1
The form of a certificate of acknowledgement used by a person shall be accepted in Ohio if any of the following apply. The certificate is in a form prescribed by the laws or regulations of...
Chapter 147 titled Notaries, Public, and Commissioners and Other Chapters of the Ohio Revised Code. Laws may and can change over time, so a best practice as a notary is to frequently check the Ohio Revised Code for changes affecting notaries public. The Ohio Revised Code empowers the Ohio Secretary of State as the governing authority who may appoint and commission notaries who meet the qualifications considered necessary to serve as notaries public.

documents.title: Columbus Ohio Notary Business Plan
documents.description: Formal Columbus launch plan with market, operating, and financial strategy.
documents.recommendedUse: Use when working on roadmap, service lanes, pricing, and growth planning.
documents.previewText:
FORMAL NOTARY BUSINESS PLAN
Columbus Ohio Notary Services
Executive Summary, Market Analysis, Financial Statements, and Capital PlanPrepared for lender / grant review
April 07, 2026
Startup cash outlay
Year 1 revenue
Year 1 EBITDA
Break-even / month
$2,114
$36,645
$20,538
13 appointments
This business plan assumes a lean, owner-operated notary practice launched in Columbus, Ohio with a hybrid model: general mobile notary, specialty mobile notary, remote online notarization (RON), selected loan signings, and recurring B2B visits.
1. Executive Summary
Columbus Ohio Notary Services is a proposed Ohio notary business designed to monetize convenience, specialty service, and repeat client relationships rather than relying only on the statutory act fee. The launch strategy uses the owner’s existing employer-based notary demand as early operating experience, then scales into mobile work, specialty bedside/title appointments, RON, and selected loan-signing activity.
The base-case model projects Year 1 revenue of $36,645, EBITDA of $20,538, pre-tax net income of $20,305, and ending Year 1 cash of $18,424. By Year 3, the model reaches projected revenue of $51,621, EBITDA of $30,936, and ending cash above $52,200. Break-even occurs at roughly 13 appointments per month under the blended base-case ticket and cost structure.
The recommended capital target is $7,500, structured primarily around startup compliance costs, working capital reserve, local marketing, and workflow automation. A bootstrapped launch is feasible because the legally required startup spend is low; however, the lender/grant-ready plan also supports a small grant or microloan if the owner wants faster rollout or more working capital.
Source note: https://codes.ohio.gov/ohio-revised-code/section-147.08https://www.ohiosos.gov/notary/become-a-notary-in-ohio/application-processhttps://www.ohiosos.gov/notary/become-a-notary-in-ohio/resources-and-frequently-asked-questions

documents.title: Ohio Notary Business Plan Financial Model
documents.description: Financial model for startup costs, revenue ramp, cash flow, and break-even planning.
documents.recommendedUse: Use when validating revenue lanes and monthly targets.
